questions.question: What is the standard user-story template?
options: As a [role], I want [goal], so that [benefit]
Given [context], When [event], Then [outcome]
Must / Should / Could / Won't
Card, Conversation, Confirmation
questions.explanation: The user-story form is "As a [role], I want [goal], so that [benefit]." Given/When/Then is the acceptance-criteria scenario format; MoSCoW is a prioritization scheme; the Three Cs describe a story's nature.

questions.question: The INVEST acronym describes the qualities of a good what?
options: A single user story
The whole Product Backlog
A Sprint Goal
A Definition of Done
questions.explanation: INVEST (Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, Testable) grades a single story. DEEP grades the whole backlog's health.

questions.question: Who is accountable for the Product Backlog's content and order, and what is its commitment?
options: The Product Owner; commitment = the Product Goal
The Scrum Master; commitment = the Sprint Goal
The Developers; commitment = the Definition of Done
The stakeholders; commitment = the release date
questions.explanation: The Product Owner is accountable for the Product Backlog (they may delegate the work, not the accountability). The backlog's commitment is the Product Goal.

questions.question: What do the "Three Cs" of a user story stand for?
options: Card, Conversation, Confirmation
Context, Cause, Consequence
Cost, Capacity, Cadence
Create, Communicate, Close
questions.explanation: Card (a short written placeholder), Conversation (where real requirements emerge), Confirmation (acceptance criteria/tests). A story is intentionally incomplete on the card — a token for a conversation, not a spec.

questions.question: Backlog refinement is one of the formal Scrum events defined by the Scrum Guide.
questions.explanation: Scrum defines exactly five events (the Sprint, Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, Retrospective). Refinement is an ongoing activity, not a mandated event — a common budget is ~10% of capacity.

questions.question: Why is the "so that" clause of a user story considered the most important part?
options: It carries the why/benefit, so the team can find a cheaper path to the same value and know when it's truly done
It specifies the database schema for the story
It assigns the story to a specific developer
It sets the story's estimate in points
questions.explanation: The "so that" makes the benefit explicit. Without it the story is a solution with no business case — you can't negotiate scope because you don't know what outcome you're protecting.

questions.question: What does the DEEP acronym describe about a Product Backlog?
options: Detailed appropriately, Estimated, Emergent, Prioritized
Defined, Estimated, Executed, Published
Detailed, Equal, Exhaustive, Permanent
Documented, Engineered, Evaluated, Planned
questions.explanation: DEEP = Detailed appropriately (top items fine-grained, bottom coarse), Estimated, Emergent (continuously re-ordered), Prioritized (a true top-to-bottom order). It grades backlog health.

questions.question: Why split stories vertically (end-to-end) rather than horizontally (by technical layer)?
options: A vertical slice cuts through all layers to deliver demoable value and hits integration risk immediately; a horizontal slice delivers no user value until the last slice lands
Horizontal slicing is faster and should be preferred
Vertical slicing avoids the need for acceptance criteria
Horizontal slicing is required by the Scrum Guide
questions.explanation: A vertical slice (UI → API → data) delivers a small, complete, demoable behavior and surfaces integration risk early. A horizontal slice ("the DB layer") delivers no value and only proves out after the last slice — violating INVEST's Valuable.

questions.question: The SPIDR technique offers patterns for splitting a too-large story. What does it stand for?
options: Spikes, Paths, Interfaces, Data, Rules
Scope, Priority, Iteration, Dependency, Risk
Story, Plan, Implement, Demo, Review
Split, Prioritize, Identify, Deliver, Refine
questions.explanation: SPIDR = Spikes (knowledge), Paths (happy vs unhappy), Interfaces (web then mobile), Data (0/1/many), Rules (simplest rule first). The spike is the only legitimately non-shippable split.

questions.question: When is a spike the appropriate response to a story?
options: When the obstacle is knowledge, not size — a timeboxed investigation that produces an answer, not a shippable increment
When the story is simply too large but well understood
When the Product Owner wants to skip acceptance criteria
Whenever a story spans more than one developer
questions.explanation: A spike addresses a genuine unknown (new API, feasibility, performance risk) that blocks estimation. It is a tightly timeboxed investigation producing knowledge — not a shippable increment. If the issue is just size, split vertically instead.

questions.question: What is the key difference between a Definition of Ready (DoR) and a Definition of Done (DoD)?
options: DoR is an optional, team-chosen entry gate (not in the Guide); DoD is the mandatory exit gate and the Increment's commitment
DoR is mandatory in the Scrum Guide; DoD is optional
Both are artifact commitments in the Scrum Guide
DoR is per-story; DoD is per-developer
questions.explanation: DoR is an optional entry gate (not in the Scrum Guide). DoD is the mandatory exit gate and the Increment's commitment. A heavy DoR risks becoming a mini-waterfall phase-gate, so keep it light or skip it.

questions.question: The 2020 Scrum Guide deliberately says the Product Backlog is "ordered" rather than merely "prioritized."
questions.explanation: "Ordered" means a single top-to-bottom sequence that folds in value, risk, dependency, size, and learning — richer than a one-dimensional high/medium/low priority label.

questions.question: How is Weighted Shortest Job First (WSJF) calculated, and why can splitting a story move it up the order?
options: WSJF = Cost of Delay ÷ Job Size; splitting shrinks job size, raising the ratio
WSJF = Job Size ÷ Cost of Delay; splitting lowers the ratio
WSJF = Reach × Impact × Confidence; splitting raises reach
WSJF = Value − Effort; splitting has no effect on it
questions.explanation: WSJF = Cost of Delay ÷ Job Size — do the most valuable, time-critical, smallest work first. Splitting a story reduces its job size (denominator), which raises its WSJF and can promote it up the order.

questions.question: A stakeholder insists every item is a "Must have." What is the most effective response?
options: Make cost of delay and trade-offs visible — cap Musts to a realistic share of capacity, force-rank within them, and let WSJF/value-vs-effort settle the order
Accept it, since the stakeholder owns prioritization
Escalate to the Scrum Master to assign the order
Mark everything as Could-have to balance it out
questions.explanation: "Everything is a Must" is an absence of priority. Cap Musts (≈≤60% of capacity), force-rank within them ("if only one ships, which?"), tie each to its "so that," and turn the argument into economics with WSJF or value-vs-effort.

questions.question: The Epic → Feature → Story → Task hierarchy is defined by the Scrum Guide.
questions.explanation: Epic/Feature/Story/Task are tooling and SAFe terms, not Scrum Guide concepts. The Guide only defines the Product Backlog and its items; an epic is simply a story too big to finish in one Sprint, and tasks live in the Sprint Backlog.

questions.question: In the Kano model, what happens to "delighter" (excitement) features over time?
options: They decay into expected basics as customers come to assume them
They become more delightful the longer they exist
They are always the highest-priority work
They never affect customer satisfaction
questions.explanation: Kano delighters decay over time — yesterday's wow becomes today's baseline expectation (a basic). That's why you must keep investing in new delighters while covering basics and performance features.

questions.question: A developer proposes splitting a large "Checkout" story into "build the DB tables," "build the REST API," and "build the React UI." What is the main problem?
options: These are horizontal (by-layer) slices — none is independently valuable or demoable until the last one lands; split vertically (e.g. checkout for one payment type end-to-end) instead
There are too few slices; it should be split into more layers
The order of the layers is wrong; UI should come first
Nothing is wrong — splitting by layer is the recommended approach
questions.explanation: By-layer slices deliver no demoable value and defer all integration risk to the end, violating INVEST's Valuable. A better split is vertical: a thin end-to-end path (e.g. checkout with one payment method, then add others).

questions.question: What is a common rule-of-thumb budget for ongoing backlog refinement, and what is its goal?
options: About 10% of capacity — enough to keep roughly one to two Sprints of work ready without over-investing in items that may change
About 50% of capacity, to fully specify the entire backlog up front
Zero — refinement should only happen during Sprint Planning
Whatever time is left after the Sprint, with no target
questions.explanation: A common budget is ~10% of capacity, keeping ~1–2 Sprints of work ready (DEEP's "detailed appropriately") without over-investing in distant items that may still change.
